---
title: "Prerequisites and Goals"
---

This page gives the overall picture of the course and the required setup.

## Prerequisites

- Basic Git / GitHub operations (covered in the previous lecture)
- A GitHub account and, ideally, the GitHub Student Developer Pack
- GitHub CLI (`gh`) installed and authenticated
  - Reference: <https://docs.github.com/en/github-cli/github-cli/about-github-cli>
  - After installation, complete authentication:

```sh
gh auth login
# In the interactive flow, choose GitHub.com -> HTTPS -> browser authentication
gh auth status   # verify that authentication worked
```

- Julia installed (`juliaup` recommended)
  - `juliaup` lets you install Julia itself and manage versions in one place
  - Reference: <https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup>
  - Example installation:

```sh
# macOS / Linux
curl -fsSL https://install.julialang.org | sh

# Windows（PowerShell）
winget install julia -s msstore
```

  - Verify installation:

```sh
julia --version
```

  - Official download page: <https://julialang.org/downloads/>

- VS Code installed with the Julia extension
  - VS Code: <https://code.visualstudio.com/>
  - Julia extension (Julia Language Support): <https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=julialang.language-julia>

- GitHub Copilot setup (students may be eligible for the Pro plan for free)
  1. **Before applying**:
     - Add your university email address to your GitHub account and make sure it is **Verified**
     - Enable **two-factor authentication (2FA)** in GitHub
     - Use an alphabet-based name on your GitHub profile
  2. **Apply for GitHub Education**: <https://education.github.com/> and request the Student Developer Pack
     - You may need to upload a student ID photo
     - Approval can take around 3 days (72 hours), so apply early
  3. **After approval**: once you receive the welcome email, enable Copilot at <https://github.com/settings/copilot/features>
  4. **Install the VS Code extensions**: GitHub Copilot and GitHub Copilot Chat
  5. Note: if you install Copilot before approval, you may hit the free-plan limit. Installing after approval is safer.

- Google Gemini Code Assist setup (a Copilot alternative; free for personal use)
  1. **Install the VS Code extension**: search for `Gemini Code Assist` in the Extensions view
  2. **Sign in with your Google account** from the Gemini sidebar panel
  3. **Use repository context**: use `@` in the chat box to add files or folders as context
  4. **If you use an IntelliJ-based IDE**: install `Gemini Code Assist` from the plugin marketplace and sign in
  - Official documentation:
    - Setup: <https://developers.google.com/gemini-code-assist/docs/set-up-gemini>
    - Chat and context selection: <https://developers.google.com/gemini-code-assist/docs/chat-gemini>
    - FAQ: <https://developers.google.com/gemini-code-assist/resources/faqs>

- Preinstall the packages used in the course if network bandwidth at the venue is limited

```sh
julia -e 'using Pkg; Pkg.add(["BenchmarkTools", "Plots", "Statistics"])'
```

  - `BenchmarkTools`: used for performance measurement
  - `Plots`: used for plotting
  - `Statistics`: provides basic statistics such as `mean` and `std`

## What This Course Aims To Teach

### Why coding still matters in the LLM era

Even if an LLM can write code, you still need to understand **abstraction and structure** to evaluate its output and give good instructions. This course is not about writing everything by hand. It is about being able to read and shape code while working with an LLM.

### Concrete goals

- Use an LLM such as VS Code Copilot or Gemini Code Assist as a working partner for Julia code
- Experience what Julia abstractions such as functions, types, and broadcast are good for
- Learn the entry point to a modern workflow based on GitHub and project environments

## Opening Question

**What does it actually mean to be able to write code together with an LLM?**
